problem_summary: 2017 MCM Problem A (continuous): the Kariba Dam on the Zambezi River faces foundation erosion and operational limits. Teams must (1) briefly assess three options — repair, rebuild, or remove-and-replace the dam with 10-20 smaller dams — with costs/benefits, then (2) do a detailed design for Option 3: a multi-dam system with the same water-management capacity and Lake Kariba protection as Kariba, addressing normal water cycles plus emergency flooding and drought, and give ZRA managers actionable guidance (number, placement, and emergency operating strategy of the dams). This O-award paper (control 65448) builds a physics-based river-flow model, selects dam sites by multi-index analysis, discretizes the river into a recurrence model, sizes the dams, and brute-forces gate-opening strategies for flood/drought.
models_used: Hydrodynamic Model (HM): a 1-D unsteady open-channel PDE system derived from first principles — a continuity (mass conservation) PDE and an energy-conservation PDE for current velocity v(x,t) and depth h(x,t) along the river, with rainfall q(x), evaporation delta(x), and a linear on-way resistance F_r = k*v; solved numerically in Mathematica with headstream boundary conditions and free-fall-distribution initial velocity from Google-Earth altitude profile y(x)

Grey Relation Grade Analysis (GRA) for dam-site selection: 8 indexes (terrain gap y'(x), stable-state depth h(x), rainfall q(x), flow acceleration dv/dt, lakes, pocket-like lowlands, valleys, river width L) over 26 candidate sites; ideal reference sequence, resolution coefficient lambda=0.5, correlation degree used to derive index weights and a weighted score Z_i; combined with k-means-style clustering to pick 11 final sites

Difference Model (DM) of the dam system: a finite-difference (discretized) reduction of the HM splitting the river into 11 dam-bounded segments, giving a 4-equation recurrence (Q'_ij, Q_(i+1)j, h_i(j+1), H_i(j+1)) that marches the river state forward in time T=60s over 90000 iterations (~3 months)

Hydraulics weir/sluice discharge law: outflow Q_(i+1)j = A_i*(H_ij - H_0i)^(3/2) with empirical outflow coefficient A_i = 2.7674*theta*b_i, giving each dam 3 discrete gate gears theta in {0.25, 0.5, 1.0}

Dam-type sizing model: 4 reservoir-size classes (I-IV) with warning water levels and cost; an iterative upgrade loop starting from cheapest type, upgrading the dam with min (H - H_warning) until all reservoirs stay below warning level AND total live storage sum(S_i*H_mi) >= V_Lake (capacity-matching constraint)

Emergency-strategy optimization via exhaustive search (brute force over all gate-gear combinations): for flooding, minimize variance of all (H_i - H_i0) subject to no dam exceeding warning level; for drought, maximize total increased downstream runoff
key_techniques: Deriving governing PDEs from control-volume mass and energy balances rather than citing a textbook open-channel (Saint-Venant) equation — shows the physics is owned, not borrowed

Sourcing real geophysical inputs: extracting the altitude profile y(x) from Google Earth and interpolating it; fitting seasonal/annual rainfall curves from World Bank hydrological-station data to get q(x)

Two-tier modeling: an expensive continuous PDE (HM) to characterize the river, then a cheap discrete recurrence (DM) for the design/optimization loop where the PDE would be intractable — explicitly justified on tractability grounds
Multi-criteria decision analysis (GRA) to fuse incommensurable continuous indexes (depth, gap) and binary topographic indexes (lake=1/0) into one objective site ranking with data-derived weights

Encoding the problem's stated requirements directly as model constraints: 'same water-management capability' -> sum(S_i*H_mi) >= V_Lake; 'protect Lake Kariba' -> minimize wet/dry-season water-level fluctuation; 'handle flood' -> minimize H-deviation variance; 'handle drought' -> maximize runoff

Anchoring the abstract model to reality: forcing dam 11 to type I because the real Cahora Bassa Dam sits there, and dam 7 to type II because post-erosion geology at the old Kariba site rules out type I
Iterative greedy sizing loop (start cheapest, upgrade the most-violating dam) instead of full combinatorial optimization over dam types

Model correction passes adding tributary inflows (q'_2,q'_3,q'_7,q'_9,q'_11 from terrain map), a collision energy-loss coefficient beta, and rainfall-driven runoff coupling Q_1j = Q_1 + alpha*(q_1 - delta_1)

Three-way directional sensitivity analysis (+/-1%, 3%, 5%) on the uncertain inputs Q_1, H_i0, h_i0, alpha for each output H_i, h_i, Q_i, surfacing that Q_i is highly sensitive to H_i0 and prescribing the mitigation (initialize H_i0 at the normal-condition stable value)
why_it_won: "From a judge's view this paper wins on physical depth plus end-to-end completeness. It does not reach for a canned river model — it derives continuity and energy-conservation PDEs from control-volume balances, which signals genuine modeling competence and is rare for an undergraduate continuous-problem entry. It then makes the smart engineering move of reducing that PDE to a fast finite-difference recurrence so it can actually iterate the design and run 90000-step simulations, demonstrating awareness of the tractability/accuracy trade-off. Every requirement in the prompt is mapped to an explicit, checkable mathematical object (capacity constraint, Lake-Kariba fluctuation, flood variance, drought runoff), so the judge can tick each box. It uses real data (Google Earth altitudes, World Bank rainfall) and grounds the abstract system in real dams (Cahora Bassa, the old Kariba site), which makes the recommendation credible rather than a toy. Finally it closes the loop with directional sensitivity analysis that not only reports robustness but diagnoses the one fragile input (H_i0) and prescribes a fix — the kind of honest, self-aware testing judges reward."
section_highlights: "Abstract is a strong 'one model per paragraph' roadmap: it names HM, GRA, DM, dam-typing, and the exhaustive emergency strategy in order, so a judge knows the whole arc before page 2. The required-deliverables restatement (section 2.1) translates each vague contest requirement into a concrete operational definition (e.g. 'water-management capability' = total live storage). The model section flows logically HM -> site selection -> DM -> dam sizing -> emergency strategy, each stage consuming the previous stage's outputs. Heavy, well-labeled use of figures (altitude profile, seasonal rainfall fits, GRA scores, candidate-site satellite map, dam diagram) and decision flowcharts for the sizing loop and the flood/drought strategy search make the algorithms readable. Tables of dam types, selected types per site (A-K), and best gate-gear combinations give concrete, actionable answers. Notations table and explicit assumptions up front; Strengths/Weaknesses and Future Work close it cleanly. Appendices carry the raw rainfall, candidate coordinates, and the full GRA metric matrix, keeping the body clean while showing the work."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The PDF-extracted result figures (Fig 7 velocity, Fig 8 depth) are clearly corrupted/placeholder in the markdown (e.g. velocity = distance as a straight 0..99 ramp, depth a symmetric triangle), so the actual numerical HM outputs cannot be verified and look implausible — a red flag if taken at face value

Many headline parameters are asserted with little justification: on-way resistance k = 1e-6, energy-loss coefficient beta = 1e-3, the runoff-rainfall coupling alpha = 3e9 (with odd/implausible units), evaporation delta = 3.2e-9 m/s, v_0 = 10 m/s; the paper itself admits k and tributary/evaporation data are guessed

River width treated as constant L = 1 km along 2000+ km of the Zambezi — a strong simplification for a river that varies enormously, and the linear-in-velocity resistance is non-physical vs. the standard quadratic (Manning/Chezy) friction

Sensitivity analysis covers only +/-5% perturbations on a few inputs; given the parameters are essentially fabricated, the true uncertainty is far larger and the 'low sensitivity' claim is overstated (and Q_i is in fact very sensitive to H_i0, 26%+)

Cost figures for Option 3 (e.g. US$860M for the dam series, type costs in Table 1) are rough order-of-magnitude estimates with weak sourcing; the three-option assessment is thin relative to the detailed Option-3 model
Geological, social, ecological, and resettlement impacts of building 11 dams are essentially ignored (acknowledged in Weaknesses), which is a serious gap for a real dam-replacement recommendation

Inconsistencies/loose ends: 'Step 1.3' mentions ten-to-twenty dams and '8 III + 2 IV' in the cost section but the final design has 11 dams with a different type mix (Table 3); the drought section mislabels delta as 'heavy rain'; the sizing flowchart's terminal logic (F -> End on 'No') is garbled
Exhaustive search over gate gears scales as 3^11 combinations — feasible here but explicitly brute-force and would not generalize to finer control or more dams
reusable_patterns: Two-model pipeline for continuous problems: build a first-principles PDE/ODE 'truth' model to understand the system, then derive a cheap discretized recurrence/difference model to actually run optimization and what-if loops — and state the tractability reason for the reduction

Derive governing equations from explicit control-volume mass+energy balances (Q_in + rain - evap - Q_out = dV) instead of quoting a named textbook equation; it reads as deeper modeling and lets you customize terms (resistance, tributaries)

Translate every fuzzy contest requirement into a concrete mathematical constraint or objective up front (capability -> storage >= V; protection -> minimize fluctuation; flood -> minimize deviation variance; drought -> maximize runoff), then satisfy them one by one
Use Grey Relation Grade Analysis to fuse mixed continuous + binary indexes into a single weighted ranking when you must choose among discrete candidate locations/options and lack ground-truth weights

Greedy iterative sizing loop: initialize at the cheapest/smallest configuration, repeatedly upgrade only the most-constraint-violating element until all constraints hold — a cheap stand-in for full combinatorial optimization

Anchor abstract model elements to real-world entities (here, forcing two dam sites/types to match existing Cahora Bassa and post-erosion Kariba geology) to boost credibility and pass the 'is this realistic?' judge test

Extract physical inputs from free public sources (Google Earth altitude profiles, World Bank hydrology) and fit smooth functions to sparse station data to get the continuous coefficient functions a PDE model needs

Run directional (+/- percentage) sensitivity tables across all uncertain inputs against all key outputs, then explicitly name the most fragile input and prescribe how to control it — turns a robustness check into actionable model hygiene
Pair each algorithm with a decision flowchart figure so reviewers can follow control flow without parsing the prose
